items.text: Workspace Cargo.toml at repo root declares members: beamr, beamr-cli
beamr crate exists at crates/beamr/ with edition 2024
beamr-cli crate exists at crates/beamr-cli/ as a binary crate with edition 2024
beamr/src/lib.rs declares public modules: atom, loader, term, process, interpreter, scheduler, gc, mailbox, supervision, native, hook, timer, module, error
beamr has no dependencies on any Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates
beamr-cli depends on beamr only
cargo check --workspace passes clean
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes clean
Global atom table implemented as a concurrent map supporting lock-free reads
Inserting a new atom string returns a unique Atom index
Inserting an already-interned atom string returns the same index
Lookup by index returns the original atom string
Concurrent inserts from multiple threads never produce duplicate entries for the same string

Common atoms pre-registered at table creation: ok, error, true, false, nil, undefined, normal, kill, EXIT, badarg, badarith, badmatch, function_clause, case_clause, if_clause, undef, badfun, badarity, noproc
Parses the .beam chunked binary container format (FOR1/BEAM header, chunk headers, chunk data)
Decodes the Atom/AtU8 chunk and registers all atoms in the global atom table
Decodes the Code chunk into a vector of internal Instruction values
Decodes the StrT chunk (string table) as a byte buffer
Decodes the ImpT chunk into import entries (module, function, arity)
Decodes the ExpT chunk into export entries (function, arity, label)
Decodes the FunT chunk into lambda entries (function, arity, label, num_free)
Decodes the LitT chunk by decompressing (zlib) and deserializing the external term format literals
Decodes the Line chunk into source location information
Decodes compact term encoding for instruction operands (tags, extended tags, literals, atoms, labels, registers)
Resolves imports against loaded modules and the BIF registry; resolved imports point to callable targets
Produces an unresolved-import report listing every import that could not be resolved, grouped by module
Validates instruction operands: register indices in range, label targets exist, arities match
Stores a successfully loaded module in the module registry by its atom name
Loading a .beam file produced by gleam build + erlc succeeds for a pure Gleam module with no external dependencies beyond erlang: built-ins
Term is a 64-bit value with low-bit tagging to distinguish types
Small integer encoded as immediate: value fits in the non-tag bits, round-trips encode/decode
Atom encoded as immediate: atom index in non-tag bits, round-trips encode/decode
Pid encoded as immediate: process id data in non-tag bits, round-trips encode/decode
Nil represented as a distinguished constant value
Tuple allocated on heap as boxed: header word with arity followed by arity element words
List cons cell allocated on heap as boxed: two words (head, tail), proper list terminates in nil
Float allocated on heap as boxed: header word followed by an f64
Binary allocated on heap as boxed: header word followed by length and byte data
Big integer allocated on heap as boxed: header word followed by sign and digit limbs
Fun/closure allocated on heap as boxed: header with module, function index, arity, and captured environment terms
Map allocated on heap as boxed: supports flatmap representation (sorted key array + value array)
Reference allocated on heap as boxed: unique id for monitors and timers
Term equality comparison (== semantics): same-type immediates compare by value, boxed terms compare structurally
Term exact equality comparison (=:= semantics): integer 1 and float 1.0 are not equal
Term ordering follows the BEAM order: number < atom < reference < fun < port < pid < tuple < map < nil < list < binary
Process struct contains: pid, heap, stack, mailbox, reduction counter, status, links, monitors, trap_exit flag, group_leader
New process starts with a small heap (233 words default)
spawn(Module, Function, Args) creates a new process, pushes the initial call frame, assigns a unique pid, and places it on a run queue
Process status transitions: new → running → (waiting | exiting), waiting → running on message or timeout
X registers (at least 256) hold function arguments and temporaries per process
Y registers are stack-allocated: allocate/deallocate instructions create/destroy stack frames with Y slots
Process exit deallocates its heap, removes it from the scheduler, and propagates exit signals to linked processes
A process that exits with reason 'normal' does not cause linked processes to exit (unless they monitor)
Each process has a unique Pid that is never reused during a VM instance
Execution loop: fetch instruction at current code pointer, execute it, advance pointer (or jump)
Reduction counter decremented on each call/apply instruction
Process yields (returns to scheduler) when reduction counter reaches zero
label instruction sets a jump target; func_info provides function metadata for error reporting
move instruction copies a term between registers, from a literal, or from a stack slot
call and call_only instructions: local function call within the same module, saving or not saving a return address
call_ext and call_ext_only instructions: call into another module or a BIF, with import resolution
call_last and call_ext_last instructions: tail call that deallocates the current stack frame before jumping
return instruction: pop the return address from the stack, jump to it
allocate and allocate_zero instructions: push a stack frame with N Y-register slots
deallocate instruction: pop the top stack frame
test_heap instruction: ensure N words of heap space available, triggering GC if not
put_list instruction: allocate a cons cell on the heap from head and tail operands
put_tuple2 instruction: allocate a tuple on the heap from an element list
get_tuple_element instruction: extract the Nth element from a tuple into a register
get_hd and get_tl instructions: extract head or tail from a cons cell
Type test instructions (is_integer, is_float, is_atom, is_list, is_tuple, is_nil, is_binary, is_boolean, is_map, is_function2): branch to fail label if test fails
is_eq_exact and is_ne_exact instructions: exact equality comparison, branch on result
is_lt and is_ge instructions: term ordering comparison, branch on result
select_val instruction: multi-way branch on a term's value against a list of value/label pairs
select_tuple_arity instruction: multi-way branch on a tuple's arity against a list of arity/label pairs
jump instruction: unconditional jump to a label
gc_bif1, gc_bif2, gc_bif3 instructions: call a guard BIF that may trigger GC, branch to fail label on error
bif0, bif1, bif2 instructions: call a guard BIF that cannot trigger GC
send instruction: send the term in x(1) to the process identified by x(0)
loop_rec, loop_rec_end, remove_message instructions: selective receive mailbox scan
wait instruction: suspend the process until a message arrives
wait_timeout and timeout instructions: suspend with a deadline, resume on message or expiry
try, try_end, try_case, try_case_end instructions: exception handling for Gleam's try expressions
raise instruction: re-raise a caught exception
badmatch, case_end, if_end instructions: generate appropriate runtime errors for failed pattern matches and case/if exhaustion
make_fun2 instruction: create a closure term capturing the current environment
call_fun instruction: invoke a closure with arguments
apply and apply_last instructions: dynamic call with module and function as terms
bs_init2 instruction: initialize a binary construction context on the heap
bs_put_integer and bs_put_binary instructions: append integer/binary segments to a binary under construction
bs_start_match2 instruction: initialize a binary matching context from a binary term
bs_get_integer2 and bs_get_binary2 instructions: extract integer/binary segments from a match context
bs_match_string instruction: match a literal byte sequence in a match context
bs_test_tail2 instruction: verify remaining bits in a match context
has_map_fields instruction: test that a map contains specified keys, branch to fail label if not
get_map_elements instruction: extract values for specified keys from a map into registers
put_map_assoc and put_map_exact instructions: create a new map by adding/updating key-value pairs
Scheduler starts N worker threads where N defaults to the number of CPU cores
Each worker thread maintains its own run queue of ready processes
Worker loop: dequeue process, set reduction budget, call interpreter, handle result (yield/wait/exit)
A yielded process is placed at the back of the run queue for re-scheduling
A waiting process is moved to the wait set and does not consume scheduler cycles
Message arrival to a waiting process moves it back to a run queue
Work stealing: an idle worker steals half the processes from the busiest worker's queue
High-priority queue: processes at high priority are dequeued before normal-priority processes
Max-priority queue: processes at max priority are dequeued before high and normal
Default reduction budget per schedule is 4000 reductions
No process runs more than one full budget without yielding, even in a tight computational loop
Dirty scheduler: a separate thread pool for long-running native function calls
A native call marked as dirty is dispatched to the dirty pool; the normal worker thread is freed immediately
Scheduler shutdown: all worker threads and dirty threads terminate cleanly when the VM shuts down
GC is per-process: collecting one process's heap does not pause or affect any other process
Generational: heap split into young generation (nursery) and old generation
Minor GC: copy live objects from young generation to old generation, reclaim young space
Major GC: copy all live objects to fresh space, reclaim both young and old
GC triggered when the process's heap cannot satisfy an allocation request
Root set includes: X registers, Y registers (stack), and mailbox terms
All term references (on stack, in registers, in mailbox) updated to point to new locations after copy
After GC, all previously reachable terms are still reachable with identical values
Heap grows on demand following a Fibonacci-like size sequence
Heap shrinks after a major GC if utilization is below a threshold
Each process has a mailbox backed by a lock-free MPSC queue
Sending a message copies the term into the receiver's heap and appends it to the receiver's mailbox
Selective receive scans the mailbox from the save pointer forward, testing each message against patterns
A matched message is removed from the mailbox and the save pointer resets to the beginning
Unmatched messages remain in the mailbox in their original order
If no message matches, the process suspends (status → waiting) and the save pointer is preserved
A new message arriving at a suspended process wakes it to re-scan from the save pointer
Multiple senders can enqueue messages to the same mailbox concurrently without data loss or corruption
link(PidA, PidB) creates a bidirectional link: both processes' link sets contain the other
When a linked process exits, an exit signal with the exit reason is sent to all linked processes
A process receiving an exit signal with a non-normal reason exits with that reason (unless it traps exits)
A process receiving an exit signal with reason 'normal' is not terminated
A process with trap_exit=true receives exit signals as {'EXIT', Pid, Reason} messages in its mailbox instead of dying
An exit signal with reason 'kill' terminates the target process unconditionally, even if it traps exits
A process killed by 'kill' propagates exit reason 'killed' (not 'kill') to its links
monitor(Pid) returns a reference and registers a monitor; the monitored process's death sends {'DOWN', Ref, process, Pid, Reason} to the monitor owner
demonitor(Ref) removes the monitor; no DOWN message is sent after demonitor
unlink(PidA, PidB) removes the bidirectional link; no exit signal is sent after unlink
Monitoring a process that has already exited immediately sends a DOWN message
Linking to a process that has already exited immediately sends an exit signal
Module registry stores loaded modules indexed by atom name
Module lookup by name returns the current version of the module or an error if not loaded
Loading a module with the same name as an already-loaded module replaces the current version
Function lookup by MFA (module, function, arity) resolves to a code pointer in the target module
Function lookup for an unloaded module or missing export returns an undef error
BIF registry maps (Module, Function, Arity) to a Rust function pointer
NIF registry maps (Module, Function, Arity) to a Rust function pointer
Native functions receive arguments as a slice of Term values and a mutable reference to the calling process
Native functions return Result<Term, Term> where Err becomes the process exit reason
Native function registration is available before any module is loaded, so BIFs are present for import resolution
A native call marked as dirty causes the process to be migrated to the dirty scheduler pool for the duration of the call
The set of required native functions is derived from the loader's unresolved-import report, not hardcoded
Minimum BIFs for Gate 1: erlang:+/2, erlang:-/2, erlang:*/2, erlang:div/2, erlang:rem/2, erlang:</2, erlang:>=/2, erlang:=:=/2, erlang:=/=/2, erlang:error/1, erlang:display/1
A configurable hook callback fires at process yield points (reduction budget exhausted or blocking on receive)
The hook receives the process pid, its current module/function/arity, and the reduction count consumed
The hook can return 'continue' (process resumes normally) or 'suspend' (process is held until explicitly resumed)
The hook callback is set per VM instance and can be changed at runtime
When no hook is registered, the hook registration slot is None and the yield path skips hook invocation
The hook cannot modify the process's registers, heap, or mailbox — it is read-only inspection plus a continue/suspend decision
Timer wheel supports scheduling, cancellation, and expiry of timeouts with O(1) insertion and cancellation
wait_timeout instruction registers a timer; if it expires before a matching message arrives, the process resumes at the timeout label
Timer is cancelled automatically when the process receives a matching message before expiry
erlang:send_after/3 BIF sends a message to a process after a delay
erlang:start_timer/3 BIF sends a {timeout, Ref, Msg} tuple after a delay
erlang:cancel_timer/1 BIF cancels a pending timer and returns remaining time
Timer resolution is millisecond-granularity or better
beamr-cli binary accepts a path to a .beam file and a module:function/arity to execute
Loads the specified .beam file via beamr::loader and spawns a process calling the specified function
Registers basic I/O natives: erlang:display/1 prints a term to stdout
Prints the exit reason when the initial process terminates
Returns exit code 0 on normal termination, non-zero on crash
Prints the unresolved-import report if loading fails due to missing imports
